_doc: Canonical default projects per GTM step. Loaded into dashboard_data.data.projects at onboarding (for start step) and at phase-change (additive for new step). Personalized by the agent based on business_profile.industry / .buyer / .product_description and (for 2.1) path_state.current_best_channel from onboarding Q10. Mirrors kpis_per_step.json structure. Each step has 6–8 projects designed to (a) advance the step's mainGoal and (b) work against the canonical bottlenecks in roadmap_defaults.json. Each project also carries 2–3 anchor `tasks` (deterministic, structural — the agent appends 1–2 user-tailored tasks at write time based on business_profile + current_best_channel + step_debrief). At write time the agent adds `n` (1-indexed), `status: 'later'` (or 'current' for the obviously first project), and `step: '<sub_id>'`.

projects.title: Run 20 discovery calls with ICP candidates and non-ICP cold prospects
projects.outcome: Call log showing which company types convert and which don't
projects.goal: ICP from observation, not from a slide
projects.why: ICP comes from observing who closes — not from a slide
tasks: Write your ICP hypothesis in 3 sentences (before the first call)
Build the candidate list: 10 ICP-match + 10 wildcard prospects
Set up a call-log template: date, company, ICP-match?, outcome, pattern note

projects.title: Write the 1-page sales playbook (ICP+/-, use-case, channel, message, motion)
projects.outcome: A playbook a stranger could read and execute
projects.goal: Repeatable means transferable
projects.why: If it's not written it's not repeatable — repeatable means transferable
tasks: Draft the 1-page playbook outline with the 5 required sections empty
Fill each section using evidence from your discovery-call log
Have a non-founder read it and try to explain the sale back to you

projects.title: Close 2 deals end-to-end using the playbook (founder-led)
projects.outcome: 2 case studies with quotes showing what worked
projects.goal: Two wins via the same playbook
projects.why: 2 wins via the same playbook IS the repeatability test — anything else is anecdote
tasks: Identify 5 ICP-match prospects from your discovery-call log most likely to close
Run each through the playbook end-to-end and log every deviation you make
After each close, write a 1-page case study with verbatim buyer quotes

projects.title: Pick 1 channel from Hormozi's Core Four and test exclusively for 30 days
projects.outcome: A decision memo: warm vs content vs cold vs paid, with 30-day evidence
projects.goal: A verdict on the lead channel
projects.why: Testing 4 channels in parallel gives no signal — 1 channel exclusively gives a verdict
tasks: Pick 1 channel based on current_best_channel evidence and write down why
Define the 30-day test: target volume, target conversion, kill criteria
Write the 30-day decision memo: keep / kill / scale, with the numbers

projects.title: Build a 5-objection handling guide
projects.outcome: 1-page script with crisp responses to the top 5 objections you hit
projects.goal: Stop relearning the same objections every call
projects.why: Without objection prep every call relearns the same lessons
tasks: Review the last 10 call recordings or notes and tag every objection raised
Pick the 5 most frequent and write a crisp 3-sentence response for each
Test the responses on 5 live calls and refine the wording weekly

projects.title: Document ICP+ and ICP- in writing
projects.outcome: A doc listing what makes a buyer qualified vs disqualified
projects.goal: Disqualification discipline
projects.why: ICP- (who NOT to sell) is the harder discipline and the bigger speed-unlock
tasks: List 5 traits of every closed-won customer and 5 traits of every painful one
Write the ICP+ / ICP- doc with 5 must-haves and 5 instant-disqualifiers
Review the next 10 inbound leads against the doc and log disqualification rate

projects.title: Build a pre-call research template
projects.outcome: A 5-field checklist used before every discovery call
projects.goal: Show up as an expert, not a tourist
projects.why: Research-prep is the difference between an expert and a tourist on the call
tasks: List 5 facts to know before every call (company, role, trigger, tools, peers)
Build the template as a Notion / Doc / sheet block you can copy per call
Spend 10 minutes per call filling the template and time-track the prep

projects.title: Hire 2 sales reps with the playbook as the job spec
projects.outcome: 2 reps onboarded with a hiring memo explaining why each was picked
projects.goal: Hire on evidence, not vibes
projects.why: Hiring without a written playbook means hiring on vibes — vibes don't ramp
tasks: Turn the 1-page playbook into a JD with skills mapped to each playbook section
Run a structured 3-stage process: screen, scenario, playbook-readback
Write a 1-page hiring memo per rep naming why they cleared each stage

projects.title: Run a 4-week structured rep onboarding cohort
projects.outcome: 4 weekly sessions + rep-knowledge quizzes proving playbook mastery
projects.goal: A curriculum, not a Slack channel
projects.why: Reps need a curriculum, not a Slack channel
tasks: Outline the 4 weekly modules: ICP, playbook, objections, deal review
Build a 10-question quiz per week and require a 90% pass to advance
Run weekly live sessions with role-play and record them for the next cohort

projects.title: Seed each rep with 10 warm intros from the founder's network
projects.outcome: 20 warm-intro at-bats logged plus first-week win signal
projects.goal: Early wins that reveal coachability
projects.why: Warm intros let new reps win early — and reveal who's coachable
tasks: List 30 warm contacts and tag which are ICP-fit
Send 20 founder-led intros (10 per rep) over week 1
Track at-bat → meeting → opportunity per rep in a shared sheet

projects.title: Hold weekly 1:1 coaching with deal-call recordings
projects.outcome: 16 recorded coaching sessions (4 per rep per month) with specific feedback
projects.goal: Compounding rep development
projects.why: Coaching is what compounds — a rep without coaching plateaus
tasks: Set a recurring 1-hour weekly 1:1 with each rep with calls reviewed in advance
Build a coaching template: 1 strength, 1 fix, 1 commitment per session
Log every coaching note in a shared doc and review monthly for patterns

projects.title: Track rep activity and bookings daily in a shared dashboard
projects.outcome: A dashboard showing calls / meetings / proposals / wins per rep
projects.goal: Spot rep problems in days, not quarters
projects.why: Without daily metrics you spot rep problems 3 months late
tasks: Define the 4 daily metrics: calls, meetings booked, proposals out, wins
Build the dashboard in your CRM or a sheet with auto-refresh per rep
Run a 10-min daily standup against the numbers each morning

projects.title: Run customer-feedback interviews 30 days post-close (5 per rep)
projects.outcome: 10 interviews confirming the rep's deals stuck and customers are happy
projects.goal: Quality of revenue, not just quantity
projects.why: A booking is not a win — a 30-day happy customer is a win
tasks: Set a 30-days-post-close calendar trigger for every closed deal
Run a 20-min interview covering: usage, expectations met, would-recommend score
Roll up findings monthly: % of deals "sticky" vs at-risk per rep

projects.title: Update playbook v2 from rep-led learnings
projects.outcome: A revised 1-page playbook incorporating what the best rep does differently
projects.goal: Playbook that scales beyond the founder
projects.why: The first playbook was founder-only — v2 is what makes it scale
tasks: Identify the top-performing rep and shadow 5 of their calls
List the 3 plays they run that aren't in v1 of the playbook
Rewrite the 1-page playbook as v2 and have all reps re-quiz against it

projects.title: Test 5 lead sources across Core Four channels
projects.outcome: A tracking sheet showing volume / cost / quality / conversion per source
projects.goal: Find the second 20% channel
projects.why: Channel #2 doesn't reveal itself without explicit testing
tasks: List 5 candidate sources spanning warm, content, cold, and paid
Allocate a fixed test budget and 30-day window per source
Log volume / cost-per-lead / SQL conversion in a shared tracking sheet

projects.title: Scale the top-performing source to >20% of pipeline
projects.outcome: A monthly playbook showing volume, cost-per-lead and rep-assignment for source #1
projects.goal: Predictable lead flow from source #1
projects.why: A winning source isn't winning until it's >20% and predictable
tasks: Document the winning source as a 1-page operating playbook
Set monthly volume + CPL targets and a rep-assignment rule
Review weekly: actual vs target volume, CPL drift, conversion stability

projects.title: Scale a second source to >20% of pipeline
projects.outcome: A documented playbook for source #2 with the same predictability
projects.goal: Halve single-channel risk
projects.why: Single-channel dependency is a strategic risk — 2 sources halves it
tasks: Pick source #2 from the test results and clone the source-#1 playbook format
Set monthly volume + CPL targets at 50% of source-#1 to start, ramp to parity
Review weekly side-by-side with source #1 to spot quality differences

projects.title: Hire a demand-gen lead with the first 3 campaigns specced
projects.outcome: 3 campaign briefs tied to ICP, core message and landing pages
projects.goal: Break the founder-led demand-gen cap
projects.why: Founder-led demand-gen caps at ~50% of needed volume — a specialist breaks the cap
tasks: Write the demand-gen JD pointing to the 2 scaled sources as proof of concept
Draft 3 campaign briefs (one per top source + 1 stretch) with ICP and message
Run a structured hiring process: screen, scenario-brief response, references

projects.title: Organize 2 sales reps into a formal pod with weekly forecasting
projects.outcome: A pod charter covering territory, lead routing and forecast cadence
projects.goal: From individual reps to a coachable system
projects.why: Pods turn individual reps into a coachable system
tasks: Write the pod charter: territory, lead routing, forecast cadence, escalation
Run a weekly 60-min pod meeting: forecast, blocker review, deal coaching
Track pod-level KPIs in a single dashboard alongside individual rep KPIs

projects.title: Add a 3rd rep and replicate the pod structure
projects.outcome: A pod-2 charter mirroring pod-1 plus first-month performance
projects.goal: Prove the playbook scales beyond pod-1
projects.why: If pod-2 won't ramp on the same playbook, the playbook isn't actually scalable yet
tasks: Copy the pod-1 charter as pod-2 with the new rep assigned
Replicate the 4-week onboarding cohort for the new rep
Compare pod-1 vs pod-2 first-month metrics and document any deltas

projects.title: Build automated lead-routing (CRM rules or Sheets formula)
projects.outcome: Automatic lead → pod assignment with SLA tracking
projects.goal: Routing scales to >50 leads/week
projects.why: Manual routing breaks at >50 leads/week — automate before it bottlenecks
tasks: Document current manual routing rules in plain English
Implement as CRM workflow or sheet formula with timestamps for SLA tracking
Run a 1-week shadow: manual vs automated, fix mis-routings before cutover

projects.title: Run a monthly demand-gen + sales alignment sync
projects.outcome: A monthly meeting covering campaign impact, lead-quality feedback and forecast
projects.goal: Keep demand-gen and sales pulling the same rope
projects.why: The most expensive failure in 2.3 is demand-gen and sales drifting apart
tasks: Set a recurring monthly 60-min calendar block with demand-gen + sales leads
Build a 4-block agenda: campaign impact, lead-quality, forecast, next-month bets
Document decisions in a 1-page recap and share within 24h

projects.title: Define your activation metric (usage event or threshold)
projects.outcome: A 1-page memo on what metric and why it's predictive of retention
projects.goal: End the "activated" debate
projects.why: "Activated" is the most overloaded word in SaaS — write down what YOU mean
tasks: List 5 candidate activation events from your usage data
Cross-tab each candidate against 90-day retention to find the most predictive
Write the 1-page memo naming the metric, threshold, and predictive evidence

projects.title: Map the Time-to-Wow onboarding journey with 5 milestones
projects.outcome: A timeline: signup → first value → second value → habit → advocate
projects.goal: Name the moment of value
projects.why: Without a named Time-to-Wow every onboarding feels acceptable
tasks: List every onboarding step from signup to month-2 active
Pick the 5 milestones that map to first value, second value, habit, advocate
Document the target time-from-signup for each milestone

projects.title: Build a weekly customer-health dashboard
projects.outcome: A dashboard: new, activated, at-risk, churned, expansion candidates
projects.goal: Customer success that compounds
projects.why: Customer success without a dashboard is intuition — and intuition doesn't compound
tasks: Define the 5 health states: new, activated, at-risk, churned, expansion
Write the rule for each state based on activation metric + usage cadence
Build the dashboard with auto-refresh and a Friday review block

projects.title: Run guided onboarding sessions with 10 new customers
projects.outcome: Notes on activation % and friction points per session
projects.goal: See what the dashboard hides
projects.why: Watching real onboardings reveals what the dashboard hides
tasks: Build an onboarding-session template with a checklist of the 5 milestones
Schedule and run 10 founder-led 45-min onboarding sessions with new customers
Log friction points per session and tag against the milestone map

projects.title: Conduct monthly churn interviews (3 lost customers per month)
projects.outcome: A churn-reason log with quotes and fix ideas
projects.goal: Capture churn signal before it freezes
projects.why: Lost customers tell you what your survey can't — only if you ask quickly
tasks: Set a calendar trigger to reach out within 7 days of every cancellation
Run 20-min interviews with a 5-question script focused on the breaking point
Log every churn-reason with quotes in a shared sheet and tag by category

projects.title: Build an expansion-interview program with 5 power users
projects.outcome: Identified upsell opportunities and feature requests for NDR growth
projects.goal: Pull NDR above 100%
projects.why: Net revenue retention is 70% of long-term enterprise value
tasks: Identify 5 power users by usage and revenue-per-customer
Run 45-min interviews focused on: what would they pay more for, who else uses it
Document 3 expansion bets and assign each to a feature, package, or seat motion

projects.title: Write the 1-page customer success playbook
projects.outcome: A guide covering first 30 days, recurring issues, success milestones and escalation
projects.goal: Day-1-ready playbook for your first CS hire
projects.why: When you hire a CS person they need the playbook on day 1, not week 6
tasks: Outline 4 sections: first 30 days, recurring issues, milestones, escalation
Fill each section using evidence from guided onboardings and churn interviews
Have a friendly customer read it and stress-test against their own experience

projects.title: Calculate blended CAC across all channels
projects.outcome: A spreadsheet showing cost-per-acquisition by source plus blended
projects.goal: One CAC number for the business
projects.why: Without blended CAC every channel feels affordable in isolation
tasks: List every customer-acquisition cost line item by channel for the last 90 days
Map each cost to the customers it produced via your tracking sheet
Compute CAC per channel and blended CAC and document the assumptions

projects.title: Measure LTV from your first 2 cohorts (12-month retention + expansion)
projects.outcome: Cohort revenue-per-customer over 12 months
projects.goal: LTV from data, not from forecast
projects.why: LTV from new customers is fantasy — LTV from cohorts is data
tasks: Pick the 2 oldest cohorts with 12+ months of data
Pull monthly revenue per customer in each cohort and sum to month-12
Calculate average LTV per cohort and note the variance

projects.title: Calculate LTV:CAC ratio and CAC-Payback period
projects.outcome: A 1-page dashboard: LTV, CAC, ratio and payback per segment
projects.goal: Three numbers that decide the business
projects.why: Three numbers decide whether you have a business or a project
tasks: Combine LTV cohort data with blended CAC to compute the ratio
Compute CAC payback = CAC / monthly gross profit per customer
Build the 1-page dashboard and review monthly with the leadership team

projects.title: Model gross margin by customer segment
projects.outcome: A P&L showing COGS, delivery cost and margin per segment
projects.goal: Find the segment that's secretly losing money
projects.why: Average gross margin hides the segment that's secretly losing money
tasks: List every cost-of-delivery line item: hosting, support, CS, third-party fees
Allocate each cost to segments based on usage or headcount
Compute gross margin per segment and rank from highest to lowest

projects.title: Identify and deprioritize lowest-margin segments
projects.outcome: A decision memo on which segments to stop pursuing and by when
projects.goal: Margin lift via subtraction
projects.why: The fastest path to higher margin is firing the worst segment
tasks: Rank segments by gross margin and flag any below your target threshold
Write a 1-page decision memo: keep / shrink / fire, with reasoning per segment
Update sales playbook ICP- and demand-gen targeting to reflect the call

projects.title: Build a unit-economics dashboard updated monthly
projects.outcome: A monthly view: MRR, CAC, LTV, churn, NRR, margin
projects.goal: Unit economics as a monthly habit
projects.why: Unit economics is a habit — review monthly or it rots
tasks: List the 6 metrics: MRR, CAC, LTV, churn, NRR, gross margin
Build the dashboard with one source-of-truth view and auto-refresh
Set a recurring monthly leadership review block and document decisions

projects.title: Run a pricing analysis vs competitors + willingness-to-pay data
projects.outcome: A research doc on whether price or package changes can lift margin
projects.goal: A pricing lever, evidenced
projects.why: A 10% price lift in SaaS often closes the entire profitability gap
tasks: Compile competitor pricing for 5 named alternatives in a comparison sheet
Run a willingness-to-pay survey or 5 interviews with current customers
Write a 1-page recommendation: hold / raise / repackage with the evidence
